options.D: The denial pauses the harness until you edit settings.local.json to allow terraform apply before continuing

questions.explanation: C denied permission call is a refusal of that specific action; the correct response is to adjust the plan, not to re-issue the identical command. C (retry) and A (one approval is permanent across sessions) are both wrong because approving a permission once is not permanent and does not whitelist future runs. D invents a hard requirement to hand-edit settings.local.json mid-flow; while settings.local.json does hold personal allow-rules, the harness does not block waiting on it after a single denial. B is correct; C and A are the confident distractors that misstate permission persistence.

questions.q: You want Claude to automatically follow a set of layout and import-ordering conventions only when touching files under `src/api/**`, without you having to restate them, and without bloating the always-on context for unrelated files. Which mechanism fits best?

options.A: A dedicated section in the root CLAUDE.md describing the api-layer conventions for src/api files
options.B: A PreToolUse hook that inspects the target file path and injects the conventions before each edit
options.C: A file in .claude/rules/ with glob frontmatter matching src/api/** that carries the conventions
options.D: A project command in .claude/commands/ that the developer manually runs before editing api files

questions.explanation: The decisive levers are path-scoped automatic application and not bloating always-on context. .claude/rules/ files with glob frontmatter are loaded only when matching paths are in play, applying conventions automatically and selectively. CLAUDE.md (A) is always-on context for the whole session, defeating the scoping goal. B PreToolUse hook (B) is real but designed to intercept/deny or run commands, not to be the idiomatic vehicle for path-based prose conventions, and it cannot guarantee the model honors injected text. B command (D) requires manual invocation, so it is not automatic. A and B are defensible but each loses on a specific lever rules satisfy directly.

questions.q: You are designing how an agent reads a customer's current account balance and account history versus how it moves money. Under MCP conventions, which mapping is correct?

options.A: Expose balance and history as tools so the model can refresh them on demand, and money movement as a resource
options.B: Expose all three as tools, because anything the model invokes by name should be modeled as a tool for consistency
options.C: Expose all three as resources, since they all concern the same account and share authentication
options.D: Expose balance and history as resources (read-only context) and money movement as a tool (side-effecting action)

questions.explanation: MCP draws the line by effect: resources supply read-only context that the host pulls in, tools perform side-effecting actions the model invokes. Balance and history are reads; moving money is a side effect (D). A inverts the semantics and makes a destructive action look like passive context. C mislabels a side-effecting transfer as read-only, hiding its risk. B collapses a meaningful safety distinction; not everything addressable should be a tool.

questions.q: A document-processing system uses one orchestrator and several subagents. Latency is dominated by the orchestrator waiting on a single subagent that summarizes a 200-page contract, while three other independent subagents finish quickly and sit idle. A dependent finalization step needs all four outputs. Which is the MOST effective NEXT step to cut end-to-end latency?

options.A: Switch the orchestrator to a faster, smaller model so it issues and collects the four subagent calls with less per-step overhead during the run
options.B: Split the long contract into sections summarized by parallel sub-subagents, then combine, so the slow path shrinks before the barrier the finalize step needs
options.C: Run the three fast subagents sequentially after the slow one so the orchestrator manages fewer concurrent calls and reduces coordination complexity overall
options.D: Remove the barrier and let the finalization step begin as soon as the three fast subagents return, incorporating the contract summary whenever it arrives

questions.explanation: The critical path is the single long summarization; the fast subagents already run concurrently, so the only way to cut end-to-end latency is to shorten that longest task. B parallelizes the slow work itself (map-reduce over sections), directly attacking the bottleneck before the necessary barrier. C serializes independent work and makes things worse. D is tempting but unsound: the finalize step genuinely depends on all four outputs, so removing the barrier risks acting on incomplete data. A targets orchestrator overhead, which is negligible compared to the 200-page summarization dominating latency.

questions.q: A trading-ops agent must place an order only if a risk check passes. The risk check is a separate tool. Today the agent calls the risk tool and the order tool, and a prompt tells it to skip the order if risk fails. Compliance now mandates that an order can NEVER be placed without a passing risk result, even under prompt-injection attempts in the input data. What is the soundest architecture?

options.A: Gate the order tool behind code that invokes it only when the risk check returns a pass, so no model decision or injected instruction can place an order otherwise
options.B: Route every order through a supervisor agent whose sole instruction is to confirm the risk check passed before allowing the order to proceed downstream
options.C: Sanitize all incoming data to strip instruction-like text before the agent sees it, preventing injected commands from altering the order decision logic
options.D: Add a strongly worded immutable system prompt forbidding orders without a passing risk check, and test it against a suite of known injection prompts

questions.explanation: Against an adversarial, irreversible action with a compliance mandate, the only sound approach is deterministic enforcement: code gates the order tool on a passing risk result, so neither the model nor any injected instruction can bypass it. A does this. D relies on prompt adherence, which injection is specifically designed to defeat; testing reduces but never eliminates the risk. B is the runner-up but is still a model enforcing the rule and is itself injection-susceptible. C (input sanitization) is useful defense in depth but cannot be proven complete and does not, by itself, make the unsafe order structurally impossible.

questions.q: Your project needs Claude to be able to query your internal ticketing system and your observability platform during sessions, exposing those as callable tools. All of the following are appropriate building blocks EXCEPT:

options.A: Using settings.local.json to keep your own personal MCP server configuration uncommitted to the repo
options.B: Defining a project command in .claude/commands/ that wraps a common multi-step ticket triage workflow
options.C: Configuring MCP servers that expose the ticketing and observability systems as tools Claude can call
options.D: Storing the API tokens for those systems as plaintext values inside the project CLAUDE.md for Claude

questions.explanation: The EXCEPT answer is D: CLAUDE.md is auto-loaded context and must never hold secrets like API tokens, since it is committed and injected into context. The other three are all legitimate: MCP servers (C) are exactly how external systems are exposed as tools; settings.local.json (A) is the right place for personal, uncommitted config including MCP setup; and a project command (B) is a fine way to package a repeatable triage workflow. The trap is that D is phrased as a convenient way to 'let Claude read the tokens,' which is precisely the anti-pattern.

questions.q: An API returns a JSON object describing a person. When a middle name is absent the model sometimes emits "middle_name": "N/A", sometimes an empty string, sometimes "none", and sometimes drops the key entirely, breaking strict downstream parsing. The other fields are fine. Which prompt change MOST directly stops the inconsistent absence formats?

options.A: Specify each field's type and state that an absent middle name MUST be the JSON literal null, with the key always present in the object
options.B: Lower the temperature parameter to 0 so that the model picks the very same absence representation on every request it handles
options.C: Append the sentence "Be consistent about how you represent any missing values" to the very end of the existing instruction block
options.D: Add a single few-shot example that shows a fully complete person record with all three of the name fields present and populated with real values

questions.explanation: A is correct: hallucinated absence formats are stopped by specifying field types and giving an explicit absence contract (always-present key, value null). D shows only a populated case, teaching nothing about the absent case. C is a vague instruction that names the goal without defining the one acceptable representation, so the model still has to guess. B reduces variance but the model may still deterministically pick a wrong representation like "N/A"; determinism is not the same as the required null contract.

questions.q: A tool-calling agent occasionally fails when a downstream API returns a 429 rate-limit error. Currently the tool wrapper catches the exception and returns the string 'Tool failed' to the model. The agent then either gives up or blindly retries identical calls. Which change will MOST improve the agent's ability to recover autonomously?

options.A: Escalate every 429 to a human operator who can decide whether the agent should pause, retry, or abandon the current task entirely
options.B: Return a structured error to the model containing the error type, that it is retryable, and the suggested wait time before the next attempt
options.C: Suppress the error entirely and have the wrapper transparently retry the call a fixed number of times before surfacing anything to the model
options.D: Instruct the agent in its system prompt to wait and retry whenever any tool returns the generic 'Tool failed' message it receives

questions.explanation: Recovery depends on the model receiving structured, actionable error context: error class, retryability, and backoff guidance let it reason about the right next action. B provides exactly that. C (wrapper-level retry with backoff) is the strong runner-up and is good practice for transient errors, but used alone it hides the failure mode from the model and cannot handle cases where the model should change strategy rather than wait; the question asks about the agent's own recovery ability. D keeps the opaque string, so the agent still cannot distinguish retryable from fatal. A removes autonomy for a routine, self-healable condition and does not scale.

questions.q: A teammate claims, "MCP is essentially a protocol for compressing and shipping model weights between hosts so tools load faster." Which statement most accurately corrects this?

options.A: MCP is a runtime that compiles tool definitions into model weights so capabilities are available without network calls
options.B: MCP is mainly a transport-security layer that enforces HTTPS and authentication between an agent and its tools
options.C: MCP standardizes an interoperable way to expose tools, resources, and prompts to models across different hosts
options.D: MCP is a model-side caching format that stores frequently used tool outputs to reduce repeated computation

questions.explanation: MCP is an open standard for interoperably exposing tools, resources, and prompts to models; it has nothing to do with weights or compression (C). B names one concern (transport security) but mislabels the protocol's purpose. D and A both repeat the weights/compression/caching misconception in different forms and are simply false descriptions of MCP.
